text: This paper is a review of the methodologies based on the cosinor procedure, which have been devised for the analysis of time series in Chronobiology. The procedure is formulated as a regression problem where it is shown that the method is generally applicable to data with a non uniform distribution in time. Through the least squares procedure, it is also possible to obtain the confidence intervals of the amplitudes, mean values and acrophases of the rhythms whose periods are a priori known. Although the Extended Cosinor Model was developed to work with short and sparse time series, it has proved to be also very useful for the analysis of longer time series with the aim of detecting the rhythms present in these data and estimating their characteristics. Basic hypotheses of the Cosinor Model are analyzed, as well as the procedures for checking them and for facing non-stationarity in the data. The suitability of these methods for the analysis of the different data sets in Chronobiology is shown, ranging from the usual studies on circadian rhythms to the characterization of the so-called chronomes, i.e., the representation of complex time patterns covering large scales of time.
